## 2. Methods

No review protocol was developed in advance of this scoping review, and the review has not been registered on PROSPERO, the Open Science Framework, or any comparable platform [REGISTRATION NUMBER — not applicable / to be supplied]. Accordingly, no protocol is available for public access [PROTOCOL URL — to be supplied]. The review team acknowledges this as a methodological limitation; prospective registration and protocol publication are recommended for future scoping reviews in this area, as they strengthen transparency, reduce the risk of post-hoc modification of eligibility criteria, and facilitate replication. The review was nonetheless conducted in accordance with the PRISMA extension for Scoping Reviews (PRISMA-ScR) reporting guidelines (Tricco et al., 2018) to maximise methodological rigour and reporting transparency.

> _**Critique:** ◯ Revision suggested (78/100). Compliance 90, faithfulness 70, scope 90, length 100._

## 2.1 Eligibility criteria

## Eligibility Criteria

Eligibility was determined using the Population, Concept, and Context (PCC) framework recommended by the Joanna Briggs Institute for scoping reviews, ensuring systematic and transparent source selection aligned with the research question.

**Inclusion criteria**

- **Population:** Studies involving undergraduate or postgraduate dental students, dental educators, or both, regardless of clinical year or programme level.
- **Concept:** Studies addressing AI tools, AI-integrated frameworks, or AI-related curriculum content in a dental education setting, including but not limited to machine learning applications, large language models, AI-assisted diagnostics, and AI-informed competency or curriculum design.
- **Context:** Studies conducted within dental education programmes globally, encompassing predoctoral, postgraduate, and continuing professional development contexts.
- **Types of sources:** Peer-reviewed publications of any design, including empirical studies (randomised controlled trials, comparative studies, cross-sectional surveys), reviews, conceptual or framework papers, and expert consensus studies, provided they present identifiable findings relevant to the research question.
- **Date range:** Publications from 2018 onwards, reflecting the period of substantive growth in AI application within health professions education. *(Assumption: to be confirmed by the review team.)*
- **Language:** English-language publications only, owing to resource constraints on translation. *(Assumption: to be confirmed by the review team.)*
- **Publication status:** Peer-reviewed journal articles only.

**Exclusion criteria**

- Studies focused exclusively on AI in clinical dental practice with no educational component — excluded as they fall outside the defined context.
- Conference abstracts, editorials, and letters to editors — excluded as they do not present sufficient methodological detail to enable data extraction.
- Studies addressing medical or allied health education without a dental-specific component — excluded as they do not satisfy the Population criterion.
- Grey literature, theses, and preprints — excluded to ensure source quality and reproducibility of retrieval. *(Assumption: to be confirmed by the review team.)*
- Duplicate publications reporting the same dataset — excluded to prevent double-counting of evidence.

Where uncertainty arose regarding eligibility, decisions were resolved through discussion and consensus among the review team; persistent disagreements were referred to a third reviewer for adjudication.

> _**Critique:** ✓ Approved (88/100). Compliance 92, faithfulness 100, scope 95, length 100._

## 2.2 Information sources

## Information Sources

Five electronic databases were searched to identify relevant literature: PubMed, Embase, Web of Science, the Cochrane Library, and ERIC (Education Resources Information Center), each searched from inception to the date of the most recent search. Grey literature was not systematically searched; Google Scholar, preprint servers, and conference proceedings were excluded on the grounds that the review was restricted to peer-reviewed journal articles, as specified in the eligibility criteria. To supplement the database searches and reduce the risk of missing relevant sources, reference lists of all included studies were screened for additional eligible publications; no targeted hand-searching of individual journal archives was undertaken beyond this snowballing procedure. The most recent search was executed on [DATE OF LAST SEARCH — to be confirmed].

> _**Critique:** ✓ Approved (88/100). Compliance 95, faithfulness 100, scope 95, length 100._

## 2.3 Search strategy

## Search Strategy

Five electronic databases were searched to identify literature relevant to the integration of artificial intelligence into dental education: PubMed/MEDLINE, Embase, Web of Science, the Cochrane Library, and ERIC (Education Resources Information Center). All databases were searched from inception to [DATE OF LAST SEARCH — to be confirmed by the review team].

The search strategy comprised two conceptual blocks corresponding to the Population and Concept elements of the PCC framework. The Population block combined controlled vocabulary and free-text terms for dental students, dental educators, and dental education programmes. The Concept block combined terms for artificial intelligence and its principal subfields — including machine learning, deep learning, neural networks, natural language processing, and large language models — with terms for curriculum, competency frameworks, and educational integration. The two blocks were combined using the Boolean operator AND. The full PubMed/MEDLINE search string, which served as the basis for translation into the syntax of each additional database, is presented below.

```
("artificial intelligence"[MeSH] OR "machine learning"[MeSH] OR "deep learning"[MeSH] OR
"neural networks, computer"[MeSH] OR "natural language processing"[MeSH] OR
"artificial intelligence"[tiab] OR "machine learning"[tiab] OR "deep learning"[tiab] OR
"large language model*"[tiab] OR "ChatGPT"[tiab] OR "generative AI"[tiab] OR
"computer-assisted diagnosis"[tiab] OR "clinical decision support"[tiab])
AND
("dental education"[MeSH] OR "education, dental"[MeSH] OR "schools, dental"[MeSH] OR
"dental students"[MeSH] OR "dental faculty"[MeSH] OR
"dental education"[tiab] OR "dental school*"[tiab] OR "dental student*"[tiab] OR
"dental curriculum"[tiab] OR "dental training"[tiab] OR "predoctoral dent*"[tiab] OR
"postgraduate dent*"[tiab] OR "oral health education"[tiab] OR "dentistry education"[tiab])
```

The following limits were applied consistently across all databases: peer-reviewed journal articles published in English from 2018 onwards. Conference abstracts, editorials, letters, theses, and preprints were excluded at the search stage where database filters permitted, and otherwise at title and abstract screening. Reference lists of all studies meeting full-text eligibility criteria were screened manually to identify additional sources not retrieved by the database searches. No targeted hand-searching of individual journal archives was undertaken. Grey literature, including Google Scholar results, institutional reports, and preprint servers, was excluded in accordance with the eligibility criteria.

> _**Critique:** ◯ Revision suggested (78/100). Compliance 75, faithfulness 100, scope 95, length 100._

## 2.4 Selection of sources

## Selection of Sources of Evidence

Identified records were imported into [Zotero/EndNote — to be confirmed by the review team], where automated duplicate detection was applied and confirmed duplicates were removed prior to screening. The remaining records were screened in three sequential stages: title screening, abstract screening, and full-text review. At each stage, two reviewers screened records independently, applying the eligibility criteria described in the preceding section. Disagreements at any stage were resolved through discussion between the two reviewers; where consensus could not be reached, a third reviewer was consulted for adjudication. Full-text articles were retrieved for all records that passed abstract screening, and the same eligibility criteria governed inclusion and exclusion decisions at full-text review. Reasons for exclusion at the full-text stage were recorded and are reported in the results. Interrater agreement was not formally quantified [INTERRATER RELIABILITY MEASURE — to be confirmed by the review team].

> _**Critique:** ◯ Revision suggested (72/100). Compliance 78, faithfulness 100, scope 75, length 100._

## 2.5 Data charting

## Data Charting Process

A structured data-charting form was developed iteratively by the review team and pilot-tested on three included studies before full application to the complete corpus, with revisions made to field definitions and response options to improve consistency and completeness.

The following categories of data were charted from each included source:

- **Bibliographic details:** title, all authors, year of publication, journal, country of corresponding author, and DOI
- **Study design:** design type, setting, intervention type, comparator (where applicable), and theoretical framework
- **Participants:** sample size, population description, country of participants, and recruitment method
- **Methods:** data collection procedures, outcome measures, and analysis approach
- **Key findings:** principal results, direction of effect, and emergent themes
- **Reported limitations:** limitations acknowledged by study authors
- **Relevance:** relevance to the research question, relevance score, and inclusion recommendation

Data charting was performed by a single charter using the structured form, with all completed charts subsequently verified by a second reviewer for accuracy, completeness, and consistency of interpretation. Disagreements between the charter and the second reviewer were resolved through discussion and consensus; no cases required referral to a third party. Where AI-assisted extraction tools were used to generate initial chart entries, all outputs were reviewed and, where necessary, corrected by a human reviewer prior to inclusion in the final dataset.

Where data were unclear or insufficiently reported within the source document, the review team attempted to resolve ambiguity through close re-reading of the full text. Direct contact with corresponding authors was not undertaken for this review, as the charted information was sufficient to address the research question across all included studies.

> _**Critique:** ✓ Approved (88/100). Compliance 95, faithfulness 100, scope 90, length 90._

## 2.6 Data items

## Data Items

Data items extracted from each included source were defined a priori and applied consistently across all 15 included studies using a structured charting form developed and pilot-tested before full application to the corpus.

**Bibliographic**
- *Title:* full title of the publication as it appears in the source journal
- *Authors:* complete author list in the order given in the publication
- *Year:* calendar year of publication
- *Journal:* name of the publishing journal
- *Country:* country of the corresponding author's institutional affiliation

**Study design**
- *Design type:* the methodological category assigned to the study (e.g. randomised controlled trial, cross-sectional survey, conceptual framework paper, Delphi consensus study)
- *Setting:* the institutional or programmatic context in which the study was conducted
- *Theoretical framework:* any named model or theory explicitly invoked to guide study design or interpretation; recorded as 'not reported' where absent

**Participants**
- *Sample size:* the number of participants who provided data for the primary outcome, as reported in the methods or results section; recorded as null for conceptual or framework papers with no empirical participants
- *Population:* the category of participants (e.g. undergraduate dental students, dental educators, mixed clinician–student cohort), including year of study where reported
- *Recruitment:* the method by which participants were identified and enrolled

**Methods**
- *Data collection:* the procedures used to generate or gather study data (e.g. survey instrument, experimental task, database search)
- *Outcome measures:* the specific variables assessed or reported as primary or secondary outcomes
- *Analysis approach:* the statistical or qualitative method applied to the data

**Findings**
- *Key findings:* the principal results as reported by the study authors, without reinterpretation
- *Effect direction:* the overall direction of the reported effect, coded as positive, negative, mixed, or not applicable
- *Themes:* conceptual or thematic categories identified within or across findings, including both author-reported and reviewer-inferred themes; inferred themes are flagged accordingly

**Limitations**
- *Reported limitations:* methodological constraints or caveats explicitly acknowledged by the study authors
- *Methodological caveats:* additional concerns regarding risk of bias or study quality noted by the reviewer during charting

Where a variable was not reported in the source document, the field was recorded as 'not reported' or null rather than inferred. No imputation of missing values was undertaken. Relevance to the research question was also charted for each source, comprising a narrative justification and a numerical relevance score, but these fields informed inclusion decisions and are not reported as substantive data items.

> _**Critique:** ✓ Approved (88/100). Compliance 95, faithfulness 100, scope 90, length 100._

## 2.7 Synthesis of results

## Synthesis of Results

The synthesis followed the methodological guidance for scoping reviews set out by Peters et al. (2020), which prioritises descriptive mapping of the available evidence rather than inferential or meta-analytic synthesis. This approach is consistent with the exploratory nature of the research question and the heterogeneity of study designs represented in the corpus.

In the first stage, the charted data were summarised descriptively to characterise the corpus. Counts and proportions were calculated for study design type, country of participants, year of publication, and population category (undergraduate students, postgraduate students, educators, or mixed cohorts). These descriptive summaries provide a quantitative overview of the scope and distribution of the evidence base.

In the second stage, thematic synthesis was conducted by iteratively reviewing the key findings, effect directions, and themes charted from each of the 15 included studies. Themes were derived inductively from the charted data rather than mapped onto a pre-specified framework, allowing patterns to emerge from the evidence. Initial thematic codes were generated by one reviewer and subsequently reviewed by a second reviewer; disagreements were resolved through discussion and consensus. Where themes recurred across multiple studies, they were grouped into higher-order categories to enable cross-study comparison.

No formal quality appraisal of included sources was undertaken. This is consistent with JBI scoping review methodology, which does not require critical appraisal as a prerequisite for inclusion, given that the purpose of a scoping review is to map the breadth of available evidence rather than to assess the strength of that evidence for clinical decision-making.

Throughout both stages, findings were interpreted in relation to the research question — how AI is being integrated into dental education curricula, competency frameworks, and accreditation standards — and mapped back to the PCC framework (Population: dental students and educators; Concept: AI tools and curriculum integration; Context: dental education programmes globally).

> _**Critique:** ✓ Approved (88/100). Compliance 95, faithfulness 100, scope 90, length 90._

## 3.1 Characteristics of included sources

## Characteristics of Included Studies

Fifteen studies met the eligibility criteria and are included in this review, published between 2022 and 2025. The corpus spans eight countries: the United States (n=6), Denmark (n=2), Australia (n=1), Canada (n=1), Germany (n=1), Peru (n=1), Qatar (n=1), and Sweden (n=1); one study (Schwendicke et al., 2023) involved an international expert panel and is attributed to Germany as the corresponding author's country of affiliation. Study designs were heterogeneous: randomised controlled trials (n=3), cross-sectional surveys (n=2), comparative experimental studies (n=2), conceptual or framework papers (n=3), a Delphi consensus study (n=1), a scoping review (n=1), an exploratory mixed-methods study (n=1), a commentary (n=1), and a simulation-based experimental study (n=1). The predominant participant population was undergraduate dental students, appearing in eleven studies; three studies additionally included or exclusively recruited dental educators, faculty, or expert panels, and one study surveyed registered dentists alongside students. Sample sizes among empirical studies ranged from 22 to 155 participants; three conceptual or framework papers reported no empirical sample. The majority of studies were conducted in single institutional settings, with the exception of the international Delphi study (Schwendicke et al., 2023) and the Australian survey (Hegde et al., 2025), which recruited across multiple sites. The included studies were published across nine journals, with the *Journal of Dental Education* contributing the greatest number of sources (n=6).

**Table 1. Characteristics of included studies**

| First author (year) | Country | Design | Population (N) | Intervention / focus | Key findings (short) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Islam et al. (2022) | USA | Conceptual framework | Faculty, students (NR) | Four Frames model for AI integration | Leadership framework for AI adoption in dental schools |
| Saghiri et al. (2022) | USA | Scoping review | Students, educators (31 studies) | AI and immersive technologies in dental education | Positive student perceptions; AI research nascent |
| Schwendicke et al. (2023) | Germany (international) | Delphi consensus | Expert panel (N=28) | Core AI curriculum for oral/dental healthcare | Four-domain curriculum; all 28 items reached consensus |
| Tadinada et al. (2023) | USA | Framework paper | Predoctoral students, educators (NR) | Technology-driven agile curriculum redesign | Four-frame organisational model for AI/VR/AR integration |
| Hultgren et al. (2023) | Sweden | Cross-sectional comparative | Dental students (N=22) | ChatGPT for Q&A and learning outcome construction | ChatGPT accurate for Q&A; learning outcomes often unsuitable |
| Johnsen & Marchini (2024) | USA | Commentary | Educators, students (NR) | ChatGPT for critical thinking outcome development | AI useful starting point; educator input essential |
| Schropp et al. (2024) | Denmark | Randomised controlled trial | Undergraduate students (N=74) | AI software (AssistDent®) for caries detection training | No sustained benefit from AI-assisted training |
| Stephan et al. (2024) | Germany | Comparative experimental | Clinical dental students (N=100) | ChatGPT for radiology report generation | AI reports error-free but shorter; students identified more findings |
| Dascalu et al. (2024) | Denmark | Simulation-based experimental | Students and dentists (N=28) | AI second-opinion framework for caries treatment planning | AI framework improved student diagnostic F1-scores |
| Fang et al. (2024) | USA | Randomised controlled trial | Predoctoral students (N=86) | Rule-based chatbot vs. Blackboard in implant clinic | Chatbot improved timeliness, engagement, and satisfaction |
| Halat et al. (2024) | Qatar | Cross-sectional survey | Undergraduate students (N=94) | AI readiness and educational needs assessment | Moderate readiness; 73% support AI curriculum inclusion |
| Brondani et al. (2024) | Canada | Mixed-methods | Students and educators (N=25) | ChatGPT for reflective assignments and thematic analysis | Instructors detected AI authorship 85% of the time |
| Chang et al. (2024) | USA | Randomised controlled trial | Third-year students (N=40) | AI-assisted full-mouth radiograph mounting | AI increased speed but reduced accuracy; over-reliance observed |
| Saravia-Rojas et al. (2024) | Peru | Comparative experimental | Undergraduate students (N=55) | ChatGPT vs. PubMed for academic writing tasks | Traditional method scored higher; students valued ChatGPT |
| Hegde et al. (2025) | Australia | Cross-sectional survey | Dentists and students (N=155) | Attitudes towards AI applications in dentistry | Majority supportive; knowledge gaps and concerns identified |

The thematic patterns emerging from these characteristics — including the dominance of tool-specific evaluations, the relative absence of accreditation-focused work, and the geographic concentration of studies — are examined in the synthesis that follows.

> _**Critique:** ◯ Revision suggested (78/100). Compliance 90, faithfulness 75, scope 85, length 75._
